critic_raw:
The design explicitly acknowledges that a stratified random split is 'unconventional given the data's temporal nature' for EHR records spanning 5 years, then defends it on the basis of 'assumed weak temporal autocorrelation in patient readmission rates.' Hospital readmission rates for chronic heart failure patients are known to shift with treatment protocol changes, drug approvals, and seasonal influenza patterns. Five years is a long span during which clinical practice evolves substantially. A random split that scrambles the temporal order ensures that training and test data come from the same time distribution — but the model will never be evaluated on its ability to generalize to a future where practice has shifted, which is the operationally relevant generalization question.

Policy changes and socio-economic shifts are 'partially controlled by incorporating them as additional covariates.' But if these covariates are only partially measured and the split is random across all 5 years, the covariates will have similar distributions in train and test regardless of their actual confounding influence. This does not control for temporal confounding — it obscures it.

The RNN is described as 'designed to capture temporal dependencies in treatment sequences,' but the feature construction description includes 'patient demographics, historical readmission patterns, and treatment timelines' without specifying how these are presented to the model. If treatment timelines are aggregated into static features (e.g., number of prior admissions, time since last admission), the RNN receives no actual sequence and the temporal modeling justification for choosing RNN over logistic regression is vacuous.

AUROC is the primary metric and is appropriate for the discrimination goal. However, model selection on AUROC without calibration assessment is problematic in clinical deployment — AUROC measures rank ordering but does not ensure that the predicted probabilities are calibrated (i.e., a predicted 30% readmission risk actually corresponds to 30% in practice), which matters for threshold-based intervention decisions.
all_issues_raised: A stratified random split across 5 years of EHR data ensures train and test come from the same time distribution, making the experiment unable to detect whether the model generalizes to future clinical practice — the operationally relevant test.

Policy and socio-economic covariates included as model inputs do not control for temporal confounding when the split is random across all years, because those covariates will have matching distributions in train and test regardless of their actual influence.

If treatment timeline features are aggregated into static inputs before being fed to the RNN, the model receives no sequence and the theoretical justification for using an RNN over logistic regression is untestable.

Model selection on AUROC without a calibration assessment (e.g., Brier score, calibration plot) produces a model optimized for rank ordering but potentially miscalibrated, undermining threshold-based clinical intervention decisions.

Policy and socio-economic covariates included as model inputs do not control for temporal confounding when the split is random across all years, because those covariates will have matching distributions in train and test regardless of their actual influence.

If treatment timeline features are aggregated into static inputs before being fed to the RNN, the model receives no sequence and the theoretical justification for using an RNN over logistic regression is untestable.

Model selection on AUROC without a calibration assessment (e.g., Brier score, calibration plot) produces a model optimized for rank ordering but potentially miscalibrated, undermining threshold-based clinical intervention decisions.
